my_sentence: Being a firefighter has a higher salary than become a teacher.
correction: Being a firefighter can give you the chance to have a higher salary than becoming a teacher.
explanation: In a 'than' comparison the two things compared must have the same grammatical form. Since the first side is a gerund idea (being a firefighter), the second side must also be a gerund: 'than becoming a teacher', not the base verb 'become'.
correct_examples: Working in a big city pays more than working in a small town.
Running a company is harder than being an employee.
context: Comparing the pros and cons of being a firefighter vs. a teacher

my_sentence: If you as a firefighter work in a big city, you can earn about 10000 to 15000 every month.
correction: If you work as a firefighter in big cities, you can earn about 10000 to 15000 every month.
explanation: Keep the verb next to its subject: 'you work as a firefighter', not 'you as a firefighter work'. The role phrase 'as a firefighter' comes after the verb, and the place phrase 'in big cities' comes last.
correct_examples: If you work as a teacher in a big city, your salary is higher.
She works as a nurse in a large hospital.
context: Explaining how a firefighter's salary depends on city size

my_sentence: If you are not work for the government, but for local contract systems, you only earn about 4000 to 7000 every month.
correction: If you are not working for the government but for local contract systems, you only earn about 4000 to 7000 every month.
explanation: Don't put 'be' directly in front of a bare verb. Use either the present continuous 'are not working' or the simple present with 'do': 'if you do not work for the government'. 'are not work' is not a valid verb form.
correct_examples: If you are not working for the government, the pay is lower.
If you do not work for a state company, the benefits are fewer.
context: Describing firefighter pay under different employers

my_sentence: Your salary only can rent an apartment in the city center.
correction: You can only rent an apartment in the city center.
explanation: The adverb 'only' goes after the modal 'can', not before it: 'can only rent', not 'only can rent'. The word order for modal + adverb is subject + modal + adverb + main verb.
correct_examples: You can only afford a small place downtown.
He can only work part-time this month.
context: Saying a firefighter's salary is barely enough for rent in the city

my_sentence: I don't want to live a place like this.
correction: I don't want to live in a place like this.
explanation: 'live' as an intransitive verb needs the preposition 'in' before the place: 'live in a place', not 'live a place'. (You only drop the preposition when 'live' takes an adverb like 'here'.)
correct_examples: I don't want to live in a place like this.
They live in a quiet town by the sea.
context: Saying he wouldn't want to live in a crowded apartment building

my_sentence: Alibaba is in Hangzhou, so I went to there many times.
correction: Alibaba is in Hangzhou, so I have been there many times. / so I went there many times.
explanation: 'there' (and 'here') already act as adverbs of place, so they take no preposition: 'go there', not 'go to there'. For a repeated experience up to now, the present perfect 'I have been there many times' is more natural than the simple past.
correct_examples: I have been there many times.
We go there every summer.
context: Talking about visiting Alibaba's headquarters in Hangzhou

my_sentence: I lived in here after I graduated from college.
correction: I've lived here after I graduated. / I've lived here since I graduated.
explanation: Don't use 'in' before 'here'/'there'; say 'lived here', not 'lived in here'. Because you still live there now, use the present perfect 'I've lived here', and 'since I graduated' fits an ongoing state better than 'after'.
correct_examples: I've lived here since I graduated.
She has worked here for ten years.
context: Explaining why he settled in Yantai after college

my_sentence: Most children like to move from countryside to cities.
correction: Most children like to move from the countryside to the city.
explanation: 'the countryside' always takes 'the' (it refers to rural areas in general as a single place), and here 'the city' is used generically as its opposite. Say 'from the countryside to the city'.
correct_examples: Many young people move from the countryside to the city.
Life in the countryside is slower than life in the city.
context: Explaining why rural schools can't pay teachers well

my_sentence: I never thought working under Tencent.
correction: I have never thought of working under Tencent.
explanation: 'think' in this sense needs the preposition 'of' before the gerund: 'think of working'. Also use the present perfect 'I have never thought' for a life experience up to now.
correct_examples: I have never thought of working abroad.
Have you ever thought of starting your own business?
context: Answering whether he'd ever consider working for Tencent

my_sentence: I think it's a game company.
correction: I think it's a gaming company.
explanation: The natural collocation for a company that makes video games is 'gaming company' (or 'video game company'). Use the '-ing' form 'gaming' as the modifier here.
correct_examples: Tencent is a huge gaming company.
She works for a gaming studio.
context: Explaining why he wouldn't work for Tencent

my_sentence: They don't have any relative job. They are not related.
correction: They're not related.
explanation: To say two people are not family, English uses 'They're not related'. 'They don't have any relative job' is not idiomatic and mixes up 'relative' (a family member / connected) with 'related' (connected by family).
correct_examples: Jack Ma and Pony Ma are not related.
Are you two related?
context: Clarifying that Jack Ma and Pony Ma are not family

my_sentence: They are founder of their company.
correction: They are founders of their company.
explanation: When the subject is plural ('they' = two people) and each is a founder, the noun must be plural too: 'They are founders'.
correct_examples: They are the founders of the company.
We are the owners of this shop.
context: Talking about the founders of Alibaba and Tencent

my_sentence: If they are not belong to the government, they can have their own businesses.
correction: If they do not belong to the government, they can have their own businesses.
explanation: 'belong' is a stative verb used in the simple present, not with 'be'. Negate it with 'do': 'do not belong', never 'are not belong'.
correct_examples: This car does not belong to me.
If they do not belong to a union, they get no support.
context: Discussing whether firefighters can run their own businesses

my_sentence: They can have enough exercise every day and keep their body health.
correction: They can have enough exercise every day and keep their body healthy.
explanation: After 'keep + object' you need an adjective describing the object's state: 'keep their body healthy'. 'health' is the noun; 'healthy' is the adjective.
correct_examples: Exercise keeps your body healthy.
Try to keep the room clean.
context: Listing the pros of being a firefighter

my_sentence: There are many patients, so you don't need to worry about fired by the hospital.
correction: There are many patients, so you don't need to worry about getting fired by the hospital.
explanation: After the preposition 'about' you need a gerund. For a passive meaning use 'getting fired' (or 'being fired'), not the bare past participle 'fired'.
correct_examples: You don't have to worry about getting fired.
He's worried about being replaced.
context: Listing the pros of being a doctor (job security)

my_sentence: If you are a doctor, you can save many lives and you can earn the trust of them.
correction: If you are a doctor, you can save many lives and earn the trust of many people.
explanation: 'them' here has no clear plural noun to refer back to (you saved 'lives', not people, in the first clause), so it sounds vague. Name who trusts you: 'the trust of many people' (or 'their trust', if 'people' was already mentioned).
correct_examples: A good doctor earns the trust of many people.
You can win the trust of your patients.
context: Listing the pros of being a doctor

my_sentence: You need to face many patients every day, and you will be very busy and tiring.
correction: You need to face many patients every day, and you will be very busy and tired.
explanation: Use the '-ed' adjective for how a person feels ('tired') and the '-ing' adjective for the thing that causes the feeling ('tiring'). A person is tired; a job is tiring.
correct_examples: You are tired after a long shift.
This job is very tiring.
context: Listing the cons of being a doctor

my_sentence: If you make a small mistake, someone may lose his or her life.
correction: If you make a mistake, your patient may lose his or her life.
explanation: 'someone' is too vague for this context; the person at risk is specifically the patient. Naming 'your patient' makes the consequence clear and precise.
correct_examples: One small mistake and your patient could die.
If the pilot errs, the passengers are at risk.
context: Explaining the high stakes of a doctor's work

my_sentence: You need to explain the reason for their families.
correction: You need to explain the reason to his or her family.
explanation: You explain something TO the person who receives the explanation, not 'for' them. Use 'explain ... to someone'.
correct_examples: The doctor explained the situation to the family.
Let me explain the rules to you.
context: Talking about telling a patient's family bad news after surgery

my_sentence: My apartment overlooked the beach.
correction: My apartment is overlooking the beach. / My apartment overlooks the beach.
explanation: This is a permanent, present fact, so don't use the simple past 'overlooked' (which suggests it no longer does). Use the present: 'overlooks the beach' (simple present for a fixed fact) or 'is overlooking the beach'.
correct_examples: My apartment overlooks the sea.
The hotel room looks out over the harbor.
context: Describing where his apartment is, near the beach

my_sentence: It is about 6000 hospital in there.
correction: In my city, there are about 6000 hospitals.
explanation: A number greater than one takes a plural noun: '6000 hospitals'. To state that something exists, use the 'there are' structure ('there are about 6000 hospitals'), and drop the preposition before 'there' ('in there' -> just name the place).
correct_examples: There are about 6000 hospitals in my city.
There are ten students in the class.
context: Estimating the number of hospitals in Yantai

my_sentence: This hospital was built by the US, and the other hospital was built by the Japan.
correction: This hospital was built by the US, and the other was built by Japan.
explanation: Most country names take no article, so it's 'by Japan', not 'by the Japan'. (A few countries with plural or descriptive names, like 'the US' and 'the Philippines', do take 'the'.) Also 'the other' alone can stand for 'the other hospital'.
correct_examples: It was built by Japan after the war.
One was made in Japan, the other in Germany.
context: Talking about which countries built local hospitals

my_sentence: For me, I only gone to three famous hospitals.
correction: I have only been to three famous hospitals. / I have only gone to three famous hospitals.
explanation: The past participle 'gone' cannot stand alone as the main verb; it needs the auxiliary 'have' for the present perfect: 'I have gone / have been to'. For places you have visited and come back from, 'have been to' is the usual choice.
correct_examples: I have been to three hospitals in this city.
She has only been to Beijing once.
context: Saying which hospitals he has visited

my_sentence: But it's not cover all.
correction: But it does not cover all of it.
explanation: For a negative simple-present action verb you need the auxiliary 'do': 'it does not cover'. 'it's not cover' (= 'it is not cover') is ungrammatical because you can't put 'be' in front of a bare verb.
correct_examples: The insurance does not cover everything.
It doesn't cover the full cost.
context: Explaining that government medical insurance is limited

my_sentence: We are an developing country.
correction: We are a developing country.
explanation: Use 'a' before a consonant sound, not 'an'. 'developing' starts with the consonant /d/, so it is 'a developing country'. (assistant note: not explicitly corrected in class, but a clear article error.)
correct_examples: China is a developing country.
It is a developing economy.
context: Explaining why the government can't cover all medical costs

my_sentence: If you work for college, your salary will higher than some people work in primary school.
correction: If you work at a college, your salary will be higher than that of people who work in primary school.
explanation: After the modal 'will' you still need 'be' before an adjective: 'will be higher', not 'will higher'. Also, to describe the people, add the relative pronoun 'who': 'people who work in primary school'. (assistant note: not explicitly corrected in class, but both are clear errors.)
correct_examples: Your salary will be higher in a big city.
Teachers who work at universities usually earn more.
context: Explaining how teachers' salaries differ by school level
